channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Region
channel.description: Uplifting stories from every corner of the world, organised by region.

item.title: Africa's Solar Boom Hits Record 54% Growth in a Single Year
item.description: Solar installations across Africa surged 54% in 2025 — a record leap driven by both utility-scale plants and rooftop systems. The Global Solar Council says this momentum is reshaping the continent's energy future, delivering affordable power to millions who need it most. With costs falling and investment accelerating, Africa is rapidly becoming a global leader in renewable energy expansion.

item.title: World Bank Mission 300 Brings Electricity to Millions Across Africa
item.description: A major new wave of World Bank funding through the Mission 300 initiative is transforming electricity access across Africa, reaching communities that have waited generations for reliable power. The initiative targets 300 million people across the continent, combining grid extensions, off-grid solar, and mini-grids to unlock economic opportunity and improve lives at unprecedented scale.

item.title: Congo Basin Bioeconomy Could Pioneer Africa's Green Transition
item.description: A new Mongabay analysis finds the Congo Basin is uniquely positioned to lead Central Africa's green transition through a people-first bioeconomy. By valuing intact forests, medicinal plants, and wildlife sustainably, local communities could generate lasting prosperity while preserving one of Earth's most vital carbon sinks. Early models show the approach can outcompete extractive industries while protecting biodiversity.

item.title: Giant Panda Downgraded From Endangered to Vulnerable — A Conservation Win
item.description: Decades of habitat protection, captive breeding, and reforestation in China have paid off: the giant panda is no longer classified as Endangered, with its status officially downgraded to Vulnerable. Wild populations have grown steadily, and panda corridor projects now link fragmented forests across Sichuan. Scientists describe it as one of the clearest proof points that targeted conservation investment can reverse extinction trajectories.

item.title: India Welcomes 9th Cheetah Litter at Kuno, Bringing Total to 38
item.description: Three new cubs born to Gamini, a South African cheetah at Kuno National Park, mark India's ninth successful cheetah litter since the world's first intercontinental carnivore reintroduction began. The country's total cheetah population has now reached 38 — a remarkable milestone for Project Cheetah, which aims to restore a species absent from India for 70 years. Local communities are celebrating the births as a symbol of ecological renewal.

item.title: Japanese Scientists Push Solar Panel Efficiency Past 130%
item.description: Researchers in Japan have achieved what was once considered theoretically impossible: solar cell efficiency exceeding 100%, reaching 130% through a multi-junction approach that harvests energy across a broader light spectrum. The breakthrough, reported in Positive News, could dramatically cut the cost and land area needed for solar installations worldwide, supercharging the global clean energy transition.

item.title: Portugal Generates Record 80.7% of Its Electricity From Renewables
item.description: Portugal produced a staggering 80.7% of its electricity from renewable sources in January 2026 — the country's best performance in nine months and a sign that its clean energy transition is firmly on track. Wind, hydro, and solar all contributed to the milestone, which places Portugal among Europe's greenest grids. Energy analysts say the record shows how quickly a medium-sized nation can transform its power system.

item.title: Paddington The Musical Sweeps Olivier Awards With Four Wins
item.description: London's West End celebrated a joyful sweep at the 2026 Olivier Awards as Paddington The Musical took home four prizes including Best New Musical, Best Director, Best Set Design, and Best Costume Design. The beloved Peruvian bear's theatrical debut delighted audiences and critics alike, proving that a story rooted in kindness, belonging, and wonder can translate beautifully from screen to stage.

item.title: France Opens One of Its First LGBTQIA+-Inclusive Senior Living Residences
item.description: A pioneering LGBTQIA+-inclusive senior living residence has opened in Lyon, France — one of the first of its kind in the country. Designed as a space of safety, dignity, and community, the residence ensures that older LGBTQIA+ individuals can age with affirmation and belonging rather than fear of discrimination. Advocates say it marks a meaningful step forward in France's commitment to inclusive eldercare.

item.title: Artemis II Crew Breaks Record for Farthest Humans From Earth — 252,757 Miles
item.description: The four-person Artemis II crew has shattered the record for the farthest any humans have ever traveled from Earth, reaching 252,757 miles (406,773 km) as they swept around the far side of the Moon. The milestone, more than 50,000 miles beyond the Apollo record, marks a defining moment in humanity's return to deep space and rekindles the spirit of exploration for a new generation.

item.title: US Adult Smoking Rate Falls to Historic Low of 9.9% — First Time in Single Digits
item.description: The proportion of US adults who smoke has dropped to just 9.9% — the lowest rate ever recorded, and the first time in history it has fallen below 10%. Decades of public health campaigns, policy interventions, and smoking-cessation support have produced this milestone. Researchers say the number translates to millions of lives extended and immeasurable suffering avoided.

item.title: Monarch Butterfly Population Soars 64% in Mexican Wintering Grounds
item.description: Mexico's monarch butterfly overwintering population grew 64% this season compared to last year — the largest colony coverage since 2018. Butterflies now occupy 7.24 acres of forest, a recovery scientists attribute to milkweed restoration, reduced pesticide use, and cross-border conservation partnerships between the US, Canada, and Mexico. The monarch's comeback is a rare, joyful win for North America's most beloved migratory insect.

item.title: Chile Protects Over 50% of Its Waters With New Giant Marine Reserve
item.description: Chile's President signed a decree creating one of the world's largest marine reserves, protecting 337,000 square kilometers around the Juan Fernández archipelago. More than 50% of Chile's waters are now under conservation — far exceeding the global 30x30 target. The reserve's Juan Fernández fur seal population, once on the brink, has rebounded to 200,000 individuals, offering vivid proof that protection works.

item.title: Chile Becomes First Country in the Americas to Eliminate Leprosy
item.description: The World Health Organization has verified Chile as the first country in the Americas — and only the second in the world — to officially eliminate leprosy as a public health problem. Decades of sustained treatment programs, early detection, and community health investment produced this landmark result. Health officials say Chile's success offers a powerful, replicable blueprint for other nations still fighting the disease.

item.title: 130+ Nations Sign Global Protections for Jaguars, Manta Rays, and Migratory Birds
item.description: More than 130 governments signed a landmark global accord at the COP15 migratory species conference held in Brazil, extending new protections to jaguars, manta rays, migratory birds, and dozens of other at-risk species. The agreement is the most expansive update to international wildlife protection in years, cementing South America's role as both the custodian and champion of the world's most extraordinary biodiversity.

item.title: Fiji Cave Holds the Largest South Pacific Bat Colony Ever Found
item.description: A Conservation International expedition exploring one of Fiji's least-visited islands discovered a cave harboring thousands of Pacific sheath-tailed bats — the largest roost of any species across the entire South Pacific. The critically endangered bats, vanishing across Oceania, have found a vital stronghold here. Scientists say the discovery is a rare beacon of hope and a call to protect the island's undisturbed habitat.

item.title: New Zealand's Stunning Te Kaha Stadium Opens in Christchurch
item.description: The One New Zealand Stadium at Te Kaha has officially opened in Christchurch with a 30,000-person capacity for sports and 37,500 for concerts. The NZ$683 million venue replaces the city's earthquake-damaged AMI Stadium and signals Christchurch's remarkable ongoing revival. Locals say the arena — already booking international acts and sporting fixtures — has transformed the city's cultural heart.

item.title: Pacific Islands Celebrate a Week of Conservation Recovery Wins
item.description: A Pacific Islands news roundup for April 2–9 documented a wave of encouraging conservation milestones across Micronesia and beyond, including astounding recovery results on Ulong Island in Palau after just one year of active restoration work, and continued progress in the Marshall Islands boosting climate resilience and ocean health. Researchers say the gains confirm that targeted, community-backed conservation in the Pacific can move fast.

item.title: Pink Rocks Reveal Hidden Jurassic Granite Mass Beneath Pine Island Glacier
item.description: Mysterious pink granite boulders perched atop Antarctica's Hudson Mountains led scientists to a stunning find: a hidden granite mass nearly 100 km wide and 7 km thick buried beneath Pine Island Glacier. By dating the rocks to the Jurassic period and matching them to gravity anomalies detected from aircraft, researchers solved a decades-old puzzle — and gained new data to improve predictions of how Antarctica's ice will respond to warming.

item.title: CSIRO Expedition Reaches Remote Cook Ice Shelf for Landmark Climate Research
item.description: A CSIRO research team sailed more than 3,000 km from Hobart to reach the poorly studied Cook Ice Shelf in East Antarctica, collecting seafloor sediment cores, ocean samples, and marine biodiversity data that stretches back hundreds of thousands of years. The expedition's haul — including a record 20.5-meter sediment core — will sharpen global climate models and deepen our understanding of how Earth's frozen south has changed over millennia.

item.title: Elbas Shift Sierra Leone's Sherbro Island From Aid to Investment
item.description: Actor Idris Elba and wife Sabrina are transforming Sherbro Island in Sierra Leone, pairing ecological sustainability with economic growth. Their initiative shifts the narrative from aid to investment, building local enterprises and eco-infrastructure. National Geographic reports the couple's vision is already drawing international attention and long-term funding to this historically overlooked island community.

item.title: African Conservation Revives Gorillas and Restores Watersheds
item.description: African-led conservation is delivering real results: a women's group in Cameroon cut farmer-herder conflicts by 62%, Tanzania communities are restoring river ecosystems with indigenous tree planting, and mountain gorilla populations have grown meaningfully. Eco-lodges now earn millions for local communities, proving that conservation and economic prosperity can flourish together when communities lead.

item.title: UN: Africa Among the World's Fastest-Growing Economies in 2026
item.description: A new UN report projects Africa's economy will grow 4.0% in 2026 and 4.1% in 2027, with several African nations ranked among the world's fastest-growing. Capital is flowing in, the working-age population is expanding, and South-South trade is growing at 9% — nearly double the global average. Africa is consolidating its place at the center of the global development story.

item.title: China Turns Factory Ruins Into an Immersive Sound Museum
item.description: A decommissioned factory in China has been transformed into the 1Y Sound Museum, where walls of reclaimed materials broadcast oral histories from former workers and local residents. Designed by 1Y Architects, the space weaves acoustic storytelling with industrial memory, creating a living cultural institution that honors labor history while inspiring community connection and creative dialogue.

item.title: Uzbekistan and ZTE Partner to Build a 6G-Ready Digital Economy
item.description: Uzbekistan's Ministry of Digital Technologies signed a landmark MOU with ZTE Corporation to accelerate the country's digital transformation. The partnership will develop high-speed broadband, robust data centers, and next-generation technologies including 6G and Wi-Fi 8. The initiative positions Uzbekistan as a rising hub for Central Asian digital innovation and opens new economic pathways for the region.

item.title: Vietnam's Sports Economy Summit Charts Path to Billion-Dollar Growth
item.description: Vietnam hosted its inaugural Sports Economy Summit 2026, bringing together government officials, investors, and industry leaders to unlock the sector's potential. Discussions covered infrastructure, media rights, tourism tie-ins, and youth development, positioning Vietnam to build a vibrant, internationally competitive sports economy. Experts say the summit marks a turning point for how the country views sport as an economic engine.

item.title: UK Renewables Top 52% for the Second Consecutive Year
item.description: Great Britain's renewables generated 52.5% of its electricity in 2025 — the second straight year above the 50% threshold. Wind power led the way, and in March 2026 alone, record combined wind and solar output saved the UK nearly £1 billion in gas imports. Clean power has stopped being the exception; it is now the country's dependable baseline.

item.title: German Village Feldheim Runs Entirely on Its Own Renewable Energy
item.description: The small German village of Feldheim has achieved complete energy independence through three decades of investment in wind turbines, biogas, and its own local grid. Fully insulated from global energy price volatility, residents enjoy stable, affordable electricity year-round. Feldheim stands as living proof that community-scale energy sovereignty is achievable — and deeply worth it.

item.title: UK Dad's Soccer Team for Bereaved Fathers Grows to 36 Members
item.description: After losing a child, one British father founded Forget-Me-Knot FC — a football club exclusively for grieving dads. Starting with six members, the team has grown to 36, united by shared loss and the healing power of sport. Each match is a tribute and a lifeline. Plans are underway to expand the club to communities across the United Kingdom.

item.title: US's Largest Offshore Wind Farm Generates Its First Clean Power
item.description: Dominion Energy's Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project — the largest offshore wind farm in the United States — has begun generating electricity for the first time, even while 30% of turbines remain to be installed. Once complete, it will power 660,000 homes and is projected to save customers $3 billion in energy costs over its first decade of operation.

item.title: PBS Kids' First Autistic Lead Character Wins Emmy for Public Service
item.description: Carl the Collector, PBS Kids' show starring its first autistic lead character, has earned an Emmy Award for Outstanding Public Service Initiative. The show offers a joyful, authentic portrayal of a collecting-obsessed kid who navigates the world on his own wonderful terms. Families and educators across the US report it has opened meaningful conversations about neurodiversity and belonging.

item.title: California's $15M Investment in Pet-Friendly Shelters Is Helping Families
item.description: California invested $15 million to make homeless shelters welcoming to pets, removing one of the biggest emotional barriers preventing people from leaving the streets — the fear of losing their animals. Early results show the program is successfully helping people transition off the streets, keeping human and animal family members together while rebuilding stability.

item.title: Chile Protects 337,000 km² in One of Earth's Largest Marine Reserves
item.description: Chilean President Boric signed a decree creating one of the world's largest no-take marine reserves around the Juan Fernández archipelago, protecting 337,000 square kilometers and placing over 50% of Chile's waters under conservation. The Juan Fernández fur seal — once near extinction — has rebounded to 200,000 individuals, proof that sustained protection can reverse even the steepest declines.

item.title: South America Named the World's Premier Travel Destination for 2026
item.description: Brazil, Argentina, Peru, and Colombia joined forces to promote South America as the planet's top travel destination for 2026, topping a global liveability ranking based on 24,000 resident surveys. A record international visitor wave is discovering the continent's extraordinary biodiversity, vibrant culture, and cuisine, injecting fresh momentum and investment into regional economies.

item.title: Latin America Weathers Global Uncertainty With Resilient Growth
item.description: Despite global trade tensions, Latin America is experiencing renewed economic momentum in 2026. Capital that left the region is returning, aided by favorable interest rates and strong reform programs in Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, and Chile. A new wave of fiscal discipline is making the region an increasingly attractive destination for long-term international investment.

item.title: Tuvalu Launches Rat Eradication on Niulakita to Restore Ecosystems
item.description: Preparations are underway on Tuvalu's Niulakita Island for a landmark invasive rat eradication project, led by the national Department of Environment with regional support from SPREP. Strong community backing and ongoing baseline data collection are setting the stage for measurable ecological recovery — revitalizing native wildlife populations and restoring traditional food resources for islanders.

item.title: Pacific Islands Unite to Unlock Green Climate Finance Through Commonwealth Hub
item.description: Over 70 government representatives from 14 Pacific small island developing states gathered in Mauritius to strengthen climate finance access. The Commonwealth Climate Finance Access Hub is helping transform local climate priorities into investor-ready projects — bridging the gap between the Pacific's urgent needs and the global capital that can fund renewable energy and coastal resilience.

item.title: Fiji Joins WTO Advisory Centre to Strengthen Its Global Trade Voice
item.description: Fiji's Cabinet approved accession to the Advisory Centre on WTO Law, equipping the island nation with improved legal capacity to navigate and defend its interests in international trade. The move is expected to expand Fiji's market participation, protect local industries, and open new economic opportunities — connecting this Pacific nation more confidently to the world trading system.

item.title: Scientists Extract Record Sediment Core, Unlocking Antarctic Climate History
item.description: Australia's CSIRO research vessel RV Investigator completed a landmark Antarctic voyage, recovering a record 20.5-meter sediment core and over 10,000 samples from the Cook Ice Shelf region. Acting as geological time capsules, these cores preserve climate records stretching back hundreds of thousands of years — a trove that will help scientists better predict how Antarctica will respond to warming.

item.title: New Study Reveals How Antarctic Winds Sculpted Earth's Ice Ages
item.description: A landmark study has revealed that Earth's most powerful ocean current — circling Antarctica, stronger than all rivers combined — formed through a complex interplay of shifting continents and ancient wind patterns. The discovery reshapes understanding of how past ice ages developed and how carbon was drawn from the atmosphere, offering vital new clues for building accurate climate models.

item.title: Africa's First Soybean Speed Breeding Facility Opens in Zambia
item.description: The International Institute of Tropical Agriculture has inaugurated Africa's first soybean speed breeding facility in Zambia, using optimized light and temperature to grow multiple crop generations per year. The breakthrough cuts new variety development from 6–8 years down to 4–5, supporting Zambia's goal of producing one million tons of soybean by 2030 and boosting food security across the region.

item.title: African-Led Conservation Keeps 90% of Wildlife Populations Stable
item.description: Across more than 8.6 million hectares of actively managed land, 90% of monitored wildlife populations are now stable or increasing. Highlights include 27 hectares of restored mountain gorilla habitat in Rwanda and a women-led initiative in Cameroon that reduced herder-farmer conflicts by 62% — proof that African-led models are redefining conservation success.

item.title: Africa's First Road Safety Charter Enters Into Force
item.description: After a decade-long campaign, the African Road Safety Charter — the continent's first legally binding road safety framework — has officially entered into force with 15 African Union ratifications. Signatory nations must now establish road safety agencies, enact laws on speeding and drink-driving, and invest in safer infrastructure to save thousands of lives.

item.title: Taiwan's Volunteer Doctors Become Asia's Quiet Humanitarian Force
item.description: Taiwan's independent medical NGOs — led by the Tzu Chi Foundation and Taiwan Root Medical Peace Corps — have mobilized over 9,000 physicians providing free care to millions worldwide. What began in 1966 with thirty housewives saving spare change in bamboo coin banks has grown into a global relief network often arriving in disaster zones before larger nations.

item.title: Thailand's Songkran Festival Draws Record 3.7 Million Travellers
item.description: Thailand's airports are preparing for a record-setting Songkran celebration, with 3.7 million passengers expected from April 10–19. Visitors from China, Japan, Malaysia, India, and South Korea are flocking to celebrate the Thai New Year, generating an estimated 30.35 billion Baht in tourism revenue — a joyful 6% rise over last year.

item.title: Manila Bay Gets Solar-Powered Plastic Interceptors
item.description: The Philippines has joined The Ocean Cleanup's 30 Cities Program, deploying solar-powered Interceptor systems in Manila Bay's waterways. Using AI and detailed river mapping, the initiative targets the rivers carrying over 90% plastic waste. Manila joins a growing global coalition aiming to eliminate one-third of all river-borne plastic reaching the ocean by 2030.

item.title: Croatia Declared Landmine-Free After 30 Years of Clearing
item.description: Croatia has officially been declared free of landmines, completing a monumental 30-year effort to clear the deadly remnants of its 1990s war. Almost 107,000 mines and 407,000 pieces of unexploded ordnance were removed at a cost of over one billion euros — closing a painful chapter and fulfilling the country's Ottawa Convention commitment.

item.title: World Crosses 10% Ocean Protection Milestone
item.description: The world has officially crossed a landmark threshold: 10% of the global ocean is now within protected or conserved areas, according to IUCN and UNEP. Over the past two years, roughly 5 million square kilometers — an area larger than the entire European Union — gained protected status, showing accelerating momentum toward the 30% by 2030 target.

item.title: 'Extinct' Large Tortoiseshell Butterfly Returns to England
item.description: The large tortoiseshell butterfly, declared extinct in Britain in the 1990s, has been spotted breeding across multiple counties in southern England. Butterfly Conservation now considers the species resident once again, with individuals photographed at Hengistbury Head in Dorset as recently as April 7 — a remarkable natural comeback after more than three decades.

item.title: US Traffic Deaths Drop to Lowest Level Since 2019
item.description: NHTSA announced that US traffic fatalities dropped 6.7% in 2025 to 36,640 — the lowest since 2019 — with the fatality rate hitting 1.10 per 100 million vehicle miles traveled, the second-lowest in US history. Fatalities decreased across 39 states, DC, and Puerto Rico, marking a meaningful nationwide win for road safety.

item.title: Harvard Finds Optimism Lowers Dementia Risk by 15%
item.description: A Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health study published in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society found that optimistic older adults had a 15% lower risk of developing dementia over 14 years. Following 9,071 cognitively healthy participants, the research offers hopeful evidence that a positive outlook may genuinely protect brain health as we age.

item.title: Massachusetts Nature Fund Initiative Heads to 2026 Ballot
item.description: The Nature for Massachusetts Coalition surpassed 100,000 signatures to place the Protect Water and Nature Initiative on the November 2026 ballot. If passed, it would dedicate over $100 million annually from existing sales tax revenue to clean water, parks, trails, and equitable access to nature across the state — a grassroots victory for conservation.

item.title: Paraguay's Holy Week Tourism Surges With 36% Revenue Jump
item.description: Paraguay's domestic tourism surged during Holy Week, with hotel occupancy hitting 90% nationwide — up 12% from 2025. Over 409,000 visitors generated $55.5 million in revenue, a 36% jump year-over-year. The candlelight procession at Tanarandy near the UNESCO-listed Jesuit missions drew 30,000 visitors alone, showcasing the country's rich cultural heritage.

item.title: Peru Restores Its Senate for First Time in 30 Years
item.description: On April 12, Peruvians headed to the polls in a historic general election that restores the country's Senate for the first time in nearly three decades, thanks to a 2024 reform. The bicameral legislature will now include 60 senators alongside 130 deputies — a major step in strengthening democratic institutions after years of political turbulence.

item.title: Colombia Hosts World's First Just Fossil Fuel Transition Summit
item.description: Colombia and the Netherlands are co-hosting the first-ever International Conference on the Just Transition Away from Fossil Fuels on April 28–29 in Santa Marta. Backed by the Belem Declaration signed by 24 nations, the conference will bring together governments, Indigenous communities, and scientists to chart equitable pathways away from fossil fuel dependence.

item.title: New Zealand Signs Landmark 30-Year Auckland City Deal
item.description: Prime Minister Christopher Luxon and Auckland Mayor Wayne Brown signed New Zealand's first-ever city deal on April 10, establishing a 30-year coordinated transport and infrastructure strategy for Auckland. The historic agreement includes priority rapid transit projects, a new Crown co-funding tool, and sets the model for future regional deals across the country.

item.title: New Zealand Funds Breakthrough Blood Cancer Treatments
item.description: New Zealand's drug-buying agency Pharmac confirmed it will fund two new combination therapies for chronic lymphocytic leukaemia starting May 1, 2026. Around 110 patients per year will gain access to venetoclax-based combination treatments as first-line options, with wider access also granted to ibrutinib regardless of mutation type — a lifeline for families across the country.

item.title: Tuvalu Launches Rat Eradication to Restore Niulakita Island
item.description: Teams from Tuvalu's Department of Environment and Island Conservation are on Niulakita Island preparing a major operation to eradicate invasive rats that damage coconut harvests and prey on seabirds. The community-backed initiative, supported by the Predator Free Pacific programme, aims to restore native ecosystems and strengthen food security for residents.

item.title: Uncharted Island Discovered in Antarctica's Weddell Sea
item.description: An international team of 93 scientists aboard the German icebreaker Polarstern stumbled upon a previously unmapped island in the Weddell Sea while sheltering from a storm. The roughly 130-metre-long island had been invisible on satellite imagery because it blended in with surrounding icebergs — a charming reminder that our planet still holds surprises waiting to be found.

item.title: Scientists Rewrite Origin of Earth's Mightiest Ocean Current
item.description: A study published in PNAS rewrites the origin story of the Antarctic Circumpolar Current, the planet's most powerful ocean flow. Scientists found that powerful westerly winds had to align with opening ocean gateways before the current could develop its climate-cooling effects — deepening our understanding of how Antarctica's isolation shaped the global climate we know today.
